howItWorks.intro: This resin calculator uses established formulas to provide accurate results.
rules: 1:1 ratio: equal parts resin and hardener
2:1 ratio: 2 parts resin to 1 part hardener
howItWorks.outro: Results are estimates based on standard formulas. Verify with current local data for your specific situation.

faq.question: How much resin do I need?
faq.answer: Calculate volume in cubic inches (L × W × D), then multiply by 0.554 for fluid ounces. For a 12×8×0.5" tray: 48 cu in = 26.6 oz of mixed resin. Always mix 10% extra to account for cups and mixing stick waste.

faq.question: What is the difference between 1:1 and 2:1 resin?
faq.answer: 1:1 resin (equal parts) is used for art resin, coating, and thin pours under 0.25 inches. 2:1 resin is typically a deep pour formula that can handle 1-2 inch depths without overheating. Always check your brand's specifications.

faq.question: Why did my resin turn yellow or cure soft?
faq.answer: Yellowing: UV exposure or using non-UV-resistant resin. Soft cure: incorrect mix ratio (most common), inadequate mixing (mix for 3-5 minutes), or curing in too-cold temperatures (below 70°F). Temperature and accuracy are critical.

faq.question: Is the Resin Calculator free to use?
faq.answer: Yes, completely free with no signup required. Use it as many times as you need — there are no limits or hidden fees.

faq.question: How accurate is this calculator?
faq.answer: This calculator uses standard crafting and DIY formulas trusted by crafters and makers. Results are reliable estimates for planning purposes. For critical decisions, we recommend consulting a qualified professional to verify.

sections.title: What Is the Resin Calculator?
sections.body: The Resin Calculator is a free online tool designed for crafters and makers who need quick, accurate calculations in the crafting and DIY space. By entering your length, width, depth, you get instant results including total resin needed, part a, part b. No formulas to memorize, no spreadsheets to build — just enter your numbers and get the answer in seconds. Whether you're a beginner or experienced professional, this calculator saves you time and eliminates guesswork.

sections.title: Why This Calculation Matters
sections.body: Getting total resin needed right can make the difference between success and costly mistakes. In crafting and DIY, small errors compound quickly. Manual calculations are error-prone and time-consuming, especially under pressure. This calculator applies proven formulas used by crafters and makers worldwide, giving you confidence that your numbers are correct. Use it to plan your projects with precision and avoid common pitfalls that trip up beginners.

sections.title: When Should You Use This Calculator?
sections.body: This tool is most useful when you know your length and need to find the right total resin needed. It's also great for quick estimates before committing to a decision, and to double-check manual calculations or professional quotes, and when comparing different scenarios side by side. Bookmark this page and come back whenever you need a fast, reliable answer — the calculator is always free and requires no signup.

howToSteps.title: Enter Your Length (inches)
howToSteps.description: Start by entering your length — this is the primary input for the calculation.

howToSteps.title: Fill In Additional Details
howToSteps.description: Complete the remaining fields: width, depth, mix ratio. Each value refines the calculation for greater accuracy.

howToSteps.title: Click Calculate
howToSteps.description: Hit the Calculate button to run the numbers. Results appear instantly below.

howToSteps.title: Review Your Results
howToSteps.description: Check your total resin needed, part a, part b. Use these figures to inform your next decision or compare against alternative scenarios.

tips: Double-check your length before calculating — even small input errors can significantly change your results.
Run the calculator with different values to compare scenarios and find the optimal approach for your situation.
Pay attention to both total resin needed and part a — they work together to give you the full picture.
Bookmark this page for quick access next time you need to plan your projects.
If you're unsure about your mix ratio, start with a conservative estimate and adjust from there.
